expectations.text: Correctly names all 7 BIS ECP elements (or clearly covers all 7 substantively)
expectations.evidence: The response names only 6 distinct elements: (1) Management Commitment, (2) Risk Assessment, (3) Export Compliance Policies, Procedures, and Guidelines, (4) Training and Awareness, (5) Screening, Auditing, and Monitoring, (6) Handling and Reporting Export Control Problems, (7) Recordkeeping. While the response does list 7 headers, Element 5 is titled 'Screening, Auditing, and Monitoring' rather than separating 'Restricted Party Screening' as a distinct element from auditing, and Element 6 is 'Handling and Reporting' (covering VSD/incident response) rather than 'Due Diligence/KYC.' The standard BIS seven elements include KYC/Due Diligence as a separate element. The response conflates restricted party screening into Element 5 with auditing, and replaces the standard Due Diligence/KYC element with an incident response element. This does not clearly cover all 7 standard BIS ECP elements as defined by BIS.

expectations.text: Identifies minimum restricted party lists to screen against (Entity List, Denied Persons, Unverified List, OFAC SDN)
expectations.evidence: Part II lists: OFAC SDN List, BIS Entity List (EAR Part 744, Supplement 4), Denied Persons List, Unverified List, and DDTC Debarred Parties List — all key required lists are identified.

expectations.text: Mentions the Consolidated Screening List (CSL) at trade.gov or single-search tool
expectations.evidence: Part II states 'Use a commercial restricted party screening (RPS) tool... that aggregates all relevant lists into a single search interface.' Step 3 of Phase 1 mentions 'at minimum, CSL screening via trade.gov.' Part IV references 'Consolidated Screening List' at export.gov/csl or trade.gov.

Element 7(a) states 'EAR Part 762 requires exporters to maintain records for five years from the date of export, reexport, or other regulated transaction.' Also mentioned in the documentation section of Part II and Element 4.

expectations.text: Addresses FDPR or semiconductor-specific export control considerations
expectations.evidence: The response does not mention FDPR (Foreign Direct Product Rule) anywhere. It mentions 3B001 and 3B002 ECCNs briefly in the product classification section and references the October 2022 semiconductor rules once in a general note. However, there is no substantive discussion of FDPR, its two-prong test, or semiconductor-specific China export controls in the context of the ECP design. The mention of 3B001/3B002 is superficial compared to what was expected.
